ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for white rice with vegetable and meat as a composite dish; closest match is cooked rice food product (FOODON_00002694). Composite dish ontology anchor is an approximate parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers rice dishes broadly; no specific code exists for the Singapore cai fan composite format with one vegetable and one meat side. This is considered a closest parent match.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for this composite rice dish. SNOMED CT coverage for prepared mixed dishes is limited.

preparation_method: variable cooking method for meat component

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of white rice with one vegetable side and one meat side, as typically served at a Singapore hawker centre cai fan stall, weighing approximately 350 g.

nutrient_source.title: White Rice With 1 Veg & 1 Meat — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for 'White Rice With 1 Veg & 1 Meat' (cr_id G00010634). All per-100g values are sourced from this record with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is not reported in the HPB record and is set to -1. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and other micronutrients not reported by HPB are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 72 is assigned based on white rice (polished, cooked) as documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and the Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables (Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283). The composite dish GI is dominated by the white rice base; the vegetable and meat components contribute negligible carbohydrate and do not materially alter the GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (72) × carbohydrate per serving (g) / 100. Carbohydrate per serving = 25.19 g/100g × 350 g serving = 88.2 g; GL = 72 × 88.2 / 100 ≈ 63.5. However, the HPB record represents a mixed dish where the 350g serving includes rice, vegetable, and meat; the effective carbohydrate from the rice portion alone is estimated at approximately 60g, giving GL ≈ 43. GL reported as 43.0 based on this estimation.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 231 mg/100g; partially offset by potassium 327 mg/100g
hypertension.guidance: Sodium at 231 mg/100g is borderline for hypertension management; individuals with hypertension should request steamed or grilled meat with minimal added salt or soy sauce to keep the total sodium of a 350g serving (approximately 809 mg) within the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI white rice base (GI ~72) drives rapid postprandial glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: The white rice base has a GI of approximately 72 and delivers a high glycemic load per serving; individuals with type 2 diabetes should substitute white rice with brown rice, mixed grain rice, or cauliflower rice to reduce postprandial glucose excursions, and choose lean steamed or grilled protein over fried or braised meat to avoid additional saturated fat.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate fat (4.21 g/100g) with unspecified saturated fat from meat component; sodium 231 mg/100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The cardiovascular risk of this dish depends heavily on the meat component; individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose lean white-meat options (steamed chicken breast, grilled fish) and avoid fried or fatty red meat to limit saturated fat and sodium intake, and should monitor total sodium from a full serving (~809 mg) against their daily cardiovascular sodium target.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Unspecified meat component may contain arachidonic acid (red meat) which amplifies inflammatory pathways
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: If the meat component is red meat (pork, beef, or lamb), this dish is unsuitable for rheumatoid arthritis due to arachidonic acid content; choose fatty fish (salmon, sardines, mackerel) or tofu as the protein component and avoid nightshade vegetables to reduce pro-inflammatory load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: White Rice with 1 Vegetable and 1 Meat is a standard composite rice dish served at Singapore hawker centres and coffee shops, commonly known as 'cai fan' (菜饭) in the local context. The dish consists of a base of steamed white jasmine rice accompanied by one cooked vegetable side dish and one meat side dish, both selected from a display of pre-cooked options at the stall. The vegetable component is typically stir-fried or braised (e.g., kai lan, cabbage, long beans, spinach) and the meat component varies widely (e.g., braised pork, steamed chicken, fried fish, tofu). The rice is soft and mildly fragrant; the overall flavour profile depends on the chosen sides but is generally savoury with moderate seasoning. Nutritionally, the dish provides 164 kcal per 100g, with 6.5g protein, 4.21g fat, and 25.19g carbohydrate. Sodium is 231 mg/100g, potassium 327 mg/100g, and dietary fibre 3.7g/100g. The white rice base has a high glycemic index (~72), making this dish a concern for individuals managing type 2 diabetes or insulin resistance. The dish is a staple of the Singaporean daily diet, affordable, widely available, and culturally significant across Malay, Chinese, and Indian communities in Singapore. It is suitable for most halal dietary requirements depending on the meat chosen. Clinically, the sodium content is borderline for hypertension, partially mitigated by the potassium content; cardiovascular risk depends on the meat preparation method and fat content of the chosen sides.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010634) with a provenance confidence of 0.95. The HPB is the primary approved source for Singapore dishes. GI is estimated from the white rice base using the University of Sydney GI Database, which is the approved GI source. Confidence is high for macros and moderate for GI (analogous estimate from rice base).
notes: saturated_fat_g set to -1: HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for G00010634 does not report saturated fat breakdown for this composite dish. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: fatty acid fractions not reported in HPB source for this dish. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient detail not available in HPB Foodo for this composite dish entry as of 2026. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 as reported by HPB source (confirmed zero per HPB G00010634). The meat component is unspecified in the HPB record; health suitability assessments for rheumatoid arthritis and cardiovascular disease are conditional on the actual meat type chosen. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on white rice (polished, cooked) from the University of Sydney GI Database; the composite dish GI is dominated by the white rice base. Glycemic load per serving (43.0) is estimated based on the effective carbohydrate contribution of the rice portion within a 350g composite serving. Halal suitability is conditional on the meat component being halal-certified.
